tldr: Headlines across all 15 country-buckets reduce this episode to a single question: will New York City enforce the ICC arrest warrant against Netanyahu when he attends the UN General Assembly in September? Fourteen of the fifteen buckets anchor on the 'arrested/not arrested' frame — Trump's vow that Netanyahu will not be detained provides the common peg across Italian wire copy, Israeli broadsheets, Spanish-language dailies, and South and Southeast Asian press alike. Egypt Independent is the sole outlier, dropping arrest language entirely to frame the story as a political fight both Trump and Mamdani may quietly welcome. Le Figaro stands apart by surfacing the legal-jurisdictional question in the headline itself, asking whether the arrest bid is 'a bluff or a real legal opportunity' — a nuance almost entirely absent from other countries' front pages.

evidence.quote: Netanyahu non sarà arrestato in nessuna circostanza
No chance Netanyahu will be arrested in New York
Netanyahu could be arrested during New York visit
Netanyahu lashes out at Zohran Mamdani over ICC arrest
Trump and Mamdani might both welcome a fight
coup de bluff ou réelle opportunité juridique ?

single_outlet_findings.finding: The only headline in the corpus to label the ICC warrant as 'bogus' — explicitly delegitimizing the arrest order at the headline layer, a framing absent from all other English and non-English titles including Israel's two outlets.

The only headline to drop arrest/warrant language entirely and frame the story as mutual political theatre, asking why both Trump and Mamdani might 'welcome a fight' — the sole political-strategy frame in the corpus.

The only headline to surface the legal-jurisdictional question explicitly, asking whether the arrest bid is 'a bluff or a real legal opportunity' — a legal-analytical framing absent from all other outlets' titles.

bottom_line: The headline layer is almost entirely a CRIME_PUNISHMENT story — 13 of 15 country-buckets lead with some variant of 'Netanyahu arrested/not arrested,' advertising this as a criminal-enforcement episode rather than a diplomatic or political one. Egypt Independent is the lone dissenter, reading the episode as mutual political theatre, while Le Figaro uniquely surfaces the jurisdictional question in its headline itself.
